segments.text: Welcome back to Robotics Update for June 22, 2026.
Today we cover AI infrastructure risks, breakthrough talks on dexterity, and advances in nanoscale touch sensing shaping the future of automation.
Let's dive into five key stories.
From Robotics & Automation News, "Opinion: The AI infrastructure risk robotics leaders can’t afford to ignore."
This report details that the advancements in the robotics sector are fundamentally tied to underlying Artificial Intelligence infrastructure and suggests industry focus on associated risks.

Current developments indicate a period of significant advancement characterized by increasingly sophisticated autonomous mobile robots, while industrial robots are demonstrating greater operational adaptability.
Specifically, AI-powered vision systems are transforming key sectors including manufacturing, logistics, and defense through these technological leaps.
Industry leaders must direct attention toward the inherent risks within this rapidly evolving AI foundation.
The necessity for robust oversight of foundational AI layers remains a central consideration for the technology sector going forward.
From Robotics & Automation News, "Interview with Sharpa’s Alicia Veneziani: ‘Dexterous manipulation is the key to useful humanoid robots’."
The report outlines that while locomotion abilities are receiving considerable focus in humanoid robotics research, experts point toward fine motor control as the more critical area for development.
Specific discussion highlighted that advanced movement capabilities represent only one component of a functional bipedal machine.
Instead, researchers identify dexterity in manipulation tasks as presenting the primary hurdle to real-world utility in humanoid systems.
This suggests that achieving high levels of fine motor precision may currently impede the deployment of these robots in diverse field settings.
Future industry efforts are expected to concentrate significant resources on advancing sophisticated grasping and object interaction protocols.
From Robotics & Automation News, "Interview with Digid’s Nils Könne and Christian Kreil: Nanoscale sensors could help solve robotics’ tactile sensing challenge."
The report details how advanced nanoscale sensors represent a promising solution to the longstanding technical hurdle of improving robotic tactile perception.
Researchers noted that for robots to operate effectively in physical settings, understanding environmental data beyond mere software processing is required.
These nano-sensors offer enhanced capability by providing detailed physical feedback, according to Könne and Kreil's discussion.
Developing these advanced physical sensing modalities is identified as a critical step toward enabling practical, real-world applications of robotics technology moving forward.
From Robotics & Automation News, "Interview with Clearpath Robotics co-founder Ryan Gariepy: ‘Most industries in Canada are under-automated’."
The report highlights a notable disparity between Canada's advanced robotic research base and its actual industrial deployment levels across various sectors.
Co-founder Ryan Gariepy indicated that most industries within the nation currently operate at levels of automation below their potential capacity.

This assessment points to a significant chasm existing between sophisticated technological capabilities developed in academic settings and their incorporation into day-to-day operational workflows across manufacturing, logistics, agriculture, mining, and defense sectors.
Addressing this gap is identified as crucial for converting established research strengths into widespread economic deployment.
From The Robot Report, "Güdel to show grinding beyond stationary robots with vertical, horizontal motion at Automate 2026."
This report details an expansion of heavy-duty robotic grinding capabilities moving past fixed setups.
Güdel has integrated two additional degrees of freedom into its existing TMV and TMF systems through this development.

These updates fundamentally change the possible scope for industrial grinding motions by enabling movement beyond traditional stationary constraints, thereby expanding versatility across various manufacturing applications.
The company plans to present these advanced, mobile grinding demonstrations at the Automate 2026 exhibition.
This advancement indicates a significant move toward greater kinematic flexibility in heavy-duty robotic process automation.
And that's your scoop for June 22, 2026!
Remember those AI risks?
Keep questioning the code in your day-to-day.
Thanks for joining us; keep building a smarter future!
Bye for now!
